agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing interacts with cancellation, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement through shipping."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that records the obligation to pay. Once created, it is considered irreversible per Finance policy."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and accepted into the system."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The moment goods leave the warehouse for delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed one second ago — PM says cancellable, Finance+Ops say not cancellable because it is already invoiced.","expected_behavior":"Undefined — depends on resolution of the contradiction."},{"scenario":"Order in picking/packing stage (pre-ship, post-placement) — same contradiction applies.","expected_behavior":"Undefined — depends on resolution of the contradiction."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not. Is the unshipped portion cancellable?","expected_behavior":"Undefined — not addressed by any stakeholder and further complicated by the invoicing contradiction."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If Finance's rule wins, customers can never cancel, leading to potential chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer attrition. If PM's rule wins, Finance must support invoice reversal (credit notes), which contradicts their stated constraint of irreversibility.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any cancellation policy that permits post-invoice cancellation forces Operations to support credit note workflows, inventory re-stocking, and pricing reconciliation — none of which are currently scoped.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry norm). If the system silently prevents cancellation due to immediate invoicing, customer satisfaction and trust will erode.","risk":"Shipping this feature with an unresolved contradiction guarantees one stakeholder's rule is violated in production. This will surface as either (a) customers unable to cancel orders they expect to cancel, or (b) Finance discovering 'irreversible' invoices being effectively reversed. Both are high-severity outcomes.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — their ability to cancel orders is the feature under design. The contradiction determines whether they can cancel at all."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Owns invoice integrity. Any resolution that permits cancellation post-invoicing requires them to accept credit notes or invoice reversal, contradicting their stated constraint."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Owns invoicing timing. Any resolution that defers invoicing breaks their pricing lock-in strategy."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Owns the customer-facing cancellation promise. A resolution that forbids cancellation post-placement negates their requirement entirely."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the frontline burden of whichever rule wins — either explaining why cancellation is impossible or processing cancellation/refund workflows."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction that makes it impossible to define correct behavior. PM says cancel before ship. Finance says never cancel after invoice. Ops says invoice happens at placement. Therefore: cancel is never possible after placement, which nullifies the PM requirement entirely. No acceptance criteria can be written until stakeholders align. Confidence is below 50 because the spec is self-contradictory.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — the three statements are formally incompatible.","Invoice and Cancellation are distinct domain concepts that interact at a lifecycle level.","Industry norm in logistics strongly favors allowing pre-shipment cancellation."],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance truly means invoices are irreversible (no credit notes allowed) or just that the original invoice record is immutable.","Whether Operations could accept deferred invoicing (e.g., invoice at shipment) without breaking pricing guarantees.","Whether a credit note or refund invoice is considered 'reversing' an invoice or is a separate acceptable financial event."],"assumptions":["All three statements are intended as hard requirements by their respective stakeholders.","'Invoicing' in the Finance and Operations statements refers to the same concept.","There is no existing credit note or invoice adjustment mechanism that stakeholders consider outside the scope of 'cancellation'."]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are formally incompatible. (1) PM: orders can be cancelled before shipment. (2) Finance: invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. (3) Ops: orders are invoiced at placement. Combining (2) and (3): orders cannot be cancelled after placement. This directly contradicts (1), which promises cancellation is available during the entire placement-to-shipment window. At least one requirement must yield.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance yields — introduce credit notes as a sanctioned reversal mechanism. Invoices remain immutable records, but a credit note can neutralize them. Cancellation is permitted pre-shipment, and a credit note is issued against the original invoice.","B: Operations yields — defer invoicing to shipment (or a later milestone). Cancellation is permitted pre-invoice. Pricing lock-in is achieved via a price-hold or quote mechanism instead of an invoice.","C: Product yields — cancellation is only available in a very narrow window before invoicing (effectively impossible given immediate invoicing). This essentially removes the cancellation feature.","D: Hybrid — allow cancellation pre-shipment but redefine it as 'cancellation + credit note' rather than 'invoice reversal'. Finance's invoice remains permanent; a new offsetting document is created. This requires Finance to accept that 'irreversible' means the invoice record persists, not that its economic effect cannot be undone."],"recommendation":"Option D (Hybrid) is the most likely resolution. In standard logistics and accounting practice, invoices are indeed immutable audit records, but credit notes are the normal mechanism to reverse their economic effect. This preserves Finance's audit trail, Operations' pricing lock-in, and the PM's cancellation promise. However, this MUST be confirmed with all three stakeholders before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria — the requirements as stated are mutually exclusive.","business_rule_broken":"PM's cancellation window (placement → shipment) is entirely negated by the combination of Finance's no-cancel-after-invoice rule and Operations' invoice-at-placement policy."}]}
